data: Front left corner of a large, well finished block of compact grey limestone. Broken to the rear, left and right sides in a diagonal break from mid-front to rear left coner. Intact top, bottom, front left. The lower face is finished smooth, like the front; The upper and left side faces are finished, but left rough picked.
Three lines of large Greek letters; all are incomplete, but the unjustified left margin suggests a text that was originally centred on the stone

Thinly incised broad letters of regular height, without serifs or marked terminations to the strokes. Alpha has straight bar; Beta has closed loops, in the second instance separated from one another. Sigma has parallel top and bottom strokes, the mid-strokes meeting slightly over halfway across.
Letters of the 3rd century BCE; the apparent double kingship reference suggests the Hieronian period
Found in foundation works for post office, between via Eumelo, via Archia, and via Carabelli
Lying on the ground near the exit gate to the area of the amphitheatre
Area archeologica della Neapolis, Orecchio di Dionisio e Teatro Greco
Archaeological park, containing the Greek theatre, altar of Hieron, Roman amphitheatre, and ancient stone quarries.
G.V. Gentili, «Nuovi elementi di epigrafia siracusana», Archivio Storico Siracusano 7 (1961): 5–25
Tauromenitana: un calendario romano di Tauromenion
Giacomo Manganaro, «Tauromenitana: un calendario romano di Tauromenion», Archeologia Classica 15 (1963): 13–31
Ricerche di antichità e di epigrafia siceliote
Giacomo Manganaro, «Ricerche di antichità e di epigrafia siceliote», Archeologia Classica 17 (1965): 183–210
Una epistola di Gerone II ai Siracusani (IG XIV.7)
Giacomo Manganaro, «Una epistola di Gerone II ai Siracusani (IG XIV.7)», Athenaeum 43 (1965): 312–20
Per una revisione dei documenti epigrafici siracusani pertinenti al regno di Ierone II

Alessia Dimartino, «Per una revisione dei documenti epigrafici siracusani pertinenti al regno di Ierone II», in Guerra e pace in Sicilia e nel Mediterraneo antico (VIII-III sec. a.C.). Arte, prassi e teoria della pace e della guerra, a c. di C. Michelini, vol. 2, 2 voll., Atti Elimi V (Pisa: Edizioni della Normale, 2006), 703–17

Facsimile Images

Apparatus criticus

  • Text of Manganaro 1977, checked against stone

English translation

Jonathan Prag

The Syracusans dedicate (this statue of) King Hieronymos, son of King Gelon, to Damater and Kore

commentary

The text is speculatively restored in several different ways by previous editors: the text of Manganaro 1977 is provided here as an example; Gentili 1961 instead suggested a dedication to Demeter and Kore by Gelon, son of Hieron. Manganaro is correct that there is theoretically space for a shorter fourth line, and the dedication to Zeus Hellanios in honour of Gelon (ISic003331 provides a plausible parallel.
